name: Robert E. F. Aler
motto: "My strength is as the strength of ten Because my heart is pure."—Tennyson. "But preaching was his chiefest talent."—Butler. (With apologies to Aler, Solomon and H. E. Greene.)
clubs: Member Glee Club, F., So., J., S.
Assistant Secretary of Y. M. C. A., S.
Maryland Scholarship, F., So., J., S.
description: He riseth up while it is yet night, and stretcheth his limbs, and parteth his hair in the middle. He looketh out upon the morning fields, and brusheth his teeth right diligently. Over a breakfast of coffee and eggs, yea, of coffee that is cold and of eggs that have known cold storage, he smacketh suburban lips, and thinketh of Latin and Physics. Above these material things his soul riseth not: verily I say unto you he should be boiled in a calorimeter until dead, and anapaests should be chanted over his body. He consulteth an Ingersoll, and seeth that it is late: he thinketh of Hopkins and hurrieth. He passeth a jitney with averted gaze; yea, the wild jitney and the young taxi tempt him not. But putting his faith in ancient things, he catcheth a Govanstown car, and hangeth thereon by the skin of his teeth. He hangeth by force to a Govanstown car; and the end thereof is destruction.

name: Ridgely W. Baer
motto: "Ther-to he strong was as a champion." — *Chaucer.*
description: It is always delightful to find a gentle and kindly soul, a sweet and loving disposition, under a rugged and repellent exterior; to meet with almost feminine tenderness coupled with Herculean thews. This is, in all probability, the combination that has made Baer one of the most popular of Hopkins undergraduates. Despite his ursine strength, of which he is excusably vain, he has the mellifluous sweetness of disposition that would better befit a Firor or Sutton than a materialistic philosopher of the post-prandial school. For Griz is always ready to fling dissecting pan and outstretched cat to the twelve winds of heaven to convince one that the world is a machine. He is well aware, we suspect, of his own almost automatic fitness to survive. It is somewhat fortunate that biology, rather than economics, is favored with the larger part of his attention. All manner of things seem to get past in the gloomy science that Baer and Darwin could prove weren't even alive at all. And as for strong men! They are sometimes put to the most degrading and subservient of uses. Baer's irony is a strange and terrible thing that has gained him a certain reputation in the select and intellectual circle of his acquaintance. A genius for low comedy added to a certain coalheaverian grace and delicacy of perception, make him a formidable, if incoherent, adversary in debate. Fencing with a scythe would doubtless seem to him the most skilled and enjoyable of recreative amusements. His attitude with regard to killing flies with howitzers is at present unknown. How will ethics blend with biology in determining the future of the embryo physician? Time alone can tell, and, though an old man and garrulous, we feel that even he might not consider the task to be quite worth the trouble.

name: Bagley, Myron E.
motto:
"His head was balled, that shoon as any glas."
—Chaucer.
"Vixi puellis nuper idoneus."
—Horace.
clubs: Vice-President Y. M. C. A., J.
description: BAGLEY won everything winnable, together with a few things that were not formerly considered so, during his first two years at Hopkins. Since then, he has been kept busy looking important and holding down the jobs given in consideration of his previous efforts. He shines brilliantly enough in classes, where learned Doctors are compelled to puzzle their bald heads deciding whether he is deferential or only impertinent. But it is in the company of such kindred spirits as Hilgartner, Joe Branham (never Jack), and the untamed Pfitsch that he is most at home. In this jovial circle it is said that Bagley casts aside the mantle of dignity that otherwise enfolds him, and shows his true high-spirited self. On the annual trip to the Eastern Shore taken by his Independent Basketball Team, too, he thaws out under the genial influence of good-fellowship, and, it has been remarked, occasionally gets a little cordial, though never to such an extent that he can be classed with the impulsive Leithiser. Bagley has worked out the spending of holidays and vacations, among other things, to a fine science. We don’t know why he is always to be found in Washington on such occasions, for, as Walter the Fernadis has remarked, “They is plenty of 'em in Balt'more, Doctor.” Be this as it may, Tingley invariably reports having spotted him in the District on such occasions. The natural question immediately suggesting itself is, of course, what was Tingley doing there? We wish Bagley luck in the Capital.

name: Nathaniel M. Beck
motto: "Integer vitae, scelerisque purus."—Horace.
description: As Beck will never by any possible chance understand and appreciate the gem of Latin verse lavished upon him just above, we see no reason why he should not continue indefinitely to be, like Fricke, contemptibly fat and happy. For the youth's strong point is not the tracking down and joyous detection of foreign idiom and classically balanced unintelligible paragraph. In fact, it has been his unenviable fate to be compelled to hang around and pursue an odd course or so every season at Summer School, as well as bang away at them during the open season, and only in this manner has he been able to take from his studies enough time for his multitudinous social engagements. It has been said of Beck that French, German, Latin and Greek are to him more than foreign. His disgracefully fat and bland countenance, however, exhibits to the most inquisitive eye no trace of the mental and physical strain under which he must constantly labor in the mad effort to conciliate the claims and combine the careers of student and polished clubman. A careful consideration of Beck engaged in the activities relative to each of these forms of indoor sport cannot fail to convince the unprejudiced mind that he would make an excellent stoker. Beck has been accused of a hyphenating tendency to give more time and attention to the distinctly limited sphere of the Y. M. C. A. than to the Class in general. Certain it is that there are many things of which he disapproves, among them being Slonimsky, Gichner, beer, tobacco and good company, but not yet, we trust, life in general. One thing remains, and that is sufficient to prove the salvation of far more doubtful characters. Beck is good-natured.

name: Frederick K. Bell
motto:
"For thou art long and lank and brown
As is the ribbed sea sand."—Ancient Mariner.
description: This is Frederick K. Bell, one of the tallest, thinnest, brownest, and all-around finest individuals whom it has ever been our remarkably good fortune to meet. Bell draws, and sings, and does at least five thousand other things, managing in his few spare moments to kill a little more tobacco than the French government annually imports from Maryland. It is about the same kind of tobacco, too. One of the strangest things about Bell is that he handles Y. M. C. A. copy for that illustrious sheet variously known as the News-Litter and News-Later. We suspect that he does this out of friendship for his boon companion, Ed. Spieker, who edits the aforementioned college weakly, and might have trouble in satisfying the purer elements among his subscribers if it were not for the lanky cub with whom we are dealing. Bell is passionately fond of music, and even goes to the Palace to hear catchy airs when the Lyric is closed to opera and Philharmonic alike. In this depraved theatre, however, he always selects a seat well toward the rear, so that he can see nothing, while hearing all. He is also fond of pretzels, among other things, and can be seen any evening in the New Howard grill disentangling their intricate windings with praiseworthy speed and diligence. Bell has probably eaten more pretzels in his misspent life than have Coffin, Vickers and Gichner put together. Incidentally, we should hate to see Coffin, Vickers and Gichner put together. Bell's friendship for Spieker has already been mentioned. It is one of the deep and lasting variety. Ed. had the measles once for a week and Bell went about with a lost expression in his doggish and devoted eyes; this disappeared only on the Editor's return.

name: Harrison C. Coffin
motto:
"He loved old books and nappy ale."—Dobson.
"For I am nothing if not critical."—Shakespeare.
clubs: Honorable Mention, F., So., Jr., S.
Member Class Debating Team, J., S.
Johns Hopkins Chapter of the International Federation of Evil Influences
description: HARRISON C. COFFIN, noticeable chiefly as to his Maxfield Parrish Sunset hair and broad Waverly brogue, developed, during his Senior year at the University, from Class misanthrope into Class dude. The head that had once loved Latin alone was crowned with a forbiddingly chaste Woodrow Wilson hat. The lips that had hurled cryptic Pictish epithets at all disturbing influences affected a disconcerting, snobbish smile. The hands that were once wont brazenly to dish beer, or finger musty volumes, were encased in rigid, what-do-I-care gloves. A crease appeared in formerly respectable trousers. It began to be whispered abroad that Coffin was in love. His friends came, one by one, to desert him. Then the true reason for the change became known, and with it returned the Baron's old prestige and sway. Coffin had pulled a little over nine in Physics I, and being thrown much, as a result, with Doc. Burka and less important members of the Faculty, was trying to live up to the part. We do not wish to create the impression, however, that emerging unscathed from a withering fire of joules and ergs is the greatest thing that Coffin ever accomplished. On the contrary, he went after the late lamented Charles Sewell, leader of all the Weeches, on the occasion of the interclass debate in his Junior year, and amazed a highly igneutral audience, composed chiefly of Weechian retainers, by a fiery display of passion and that which is punch. It would be well to mention in passing that Coffin is a member in high standing of the Johns Hopkins Chapter of the International Federation of Evil Influences.

name: Guy N. Cromwell
motto: "Yes, think it and wish it as much as you will, But do it? No, that I will never."—Ibsen.
description: Once upon a time, we shouldn't dare to say how long ago, a wee but sturdy male babelet (the last, in preference to the prevalent "kiddie"), was born in the placid village of Glenburnie, Anne Arundel County, Maryland. The village didn't suspect at the time that a pillar of predestination had been erected in its midst, but Guy Nelson isn't the sort that can be concealed indefinitely, so it came to be whispered that a religious element had entered the community—an event as much to be deplored as the advent of a Republican. The aforementioned man-child waxed stronger and yet more strong till the time came for him to enter Johns Hopkins, and we just happened to be entering, ourselves. Hence our acquaintance. But not hence Guy. Guy is really a reincarnation of old Oliver of the same name, who has been allowed by the gods to try a come-back, his first life being admittedly something of a fluke. We think the second rather an improvement. Although some of the old traits that have persisted might most profitably have been dropped. You see, Guy attends classes religiously, and he has never been known to smile, and his silent aloofness from such carnal delights as women, tobacco and chewing gum, is a bit irritating. Let the Board hasten to explain that it couldn't help liking Cromwell, even if it wanted to. And what an ideal Family Physician he'll be.

name: Dryden, Hugh Latimer
motto: "No branch they say of all philosophy So deep, abstruse, he has not mastered it, Learned as few are learned." —Poe. "And still the wonder grew That one small head could carry all he knew." —Goldsmith.
description: DRYDEN has a peculiarly pernicious habit of salting down a 10 in every course which he favors with his attention, and he never favors anything but courses with said faculty. He is a scientist. Far be it from this talented youth to waste the golden hours taking anything frivolously effeminate, like languages or Magreeneian conversation. He is attracted only by what certain individuals, headed by Burka and the sainted Joe, refer to familiarly as "practical sciences." He might experience some difficulty in differentiating Wordsworth's treatment of Nature from Tennyson's, but ask him about the Mass Law, or Ionization, and he will discourse learnedly for hours, and discourse altogether for weeks. Mathematics 4 is his idea of a pleasant, agreeable, though rather light and easy, elective. Yet, with all this, Dryden does not cultivate the sardonic disposition peculiar to scientists and mathematicians too influential to mention. He might, on the contrary, be taken for Sunny Jim's younger, very much younger, brother. For Dryden's habitual physiognomatic expression is a grin—not a smile, nor a smirk, nor eke a facial ripple, but an undoubted, unqualified and an unmitigated grin. We suspect that he likes himself. Dryden graduated in three years—hence we don't know him as well as we should like to. The opinion held by his former Class regarding him was, however, wholly favorable, and this fact, considering the unfortunate unpopularity of the drab virtues and the midnight oil, is more or less remarkable and wholly to Dryden's credit.

name: Ira B. Fader
motto: "I, thus neglecting worldly ends, am dedicated To closeness and the bettering of my mind." —Shakespeare.
description: The panatella-shaped growth whose likeness is recorded in the West by Northwest corner of the present sheet is ordinarily concealed behind a pair of tortoise cheaters. A handful of fried oysters, of which he is passionately fond, however, proved effectual in luring him out to be photographed, the result of the experiment resembling a Post-Impressionistic daguerreotype of Dawn in a Valley. Under ordinary circumstances, Ira wouldn't have graduated until next year, if at all. But, fired by the inspirational quality of the uncensored lectures of one H. Slonimsky, Polish refugee plenipotentiary extraordinary (Warsaw papers please copy), and hearing how very, very few of the present Graduating Class were really going to graduate, after all, Brownold threw in his lot with a group of young men already sufficiently distinguished, and it may be said to his credit that he has never regretted it. Fader's strong point is Paleontology, though he has won for himself no small degree of fame in other fields, notably that of English History. To the question as to what Paleontology is, the Board can only return an ignorant but respectful shrug of the shoulders. We have been led to believe, however, that the field is promising and comparatively uncrowded. We can further state with authority that Ira once electrified the scientific world by finding a bone which a Paleont. Professor swore was the tooth of a mammoth, but which a coroner's jury of twelve butchers later identified as a sheep-shank.

name: Fricke, Robert Elmer
motto: "Portly product of beef and beer."—Dobson.
clubs: Member Varsity Football Squad, F., So., J.
description: FRICKE is commonly known as "Camel," not because he could, or would, under the most pressing circumstances, go eight days without a drink, but only because, after giving up cigarettes in his Junior year, he discovered a milkweedian substitute masquerading under this placid name, and took it unto himself with avidity and greed. The two have been living happily together ever afterward. Fricke played football for three years, but modestly withdrew for his Senior season, whereupon the greatly strengthened team paraded to the State title. Not that Camel is lacking in weight, or breadth, or courage. He is simply too good-natured to tackle an opposing runner. He would sooner settle the dispute over a reeking pipe in his room at the Y. M. C. A., or behind a dish of "Arrow collar" stuff at Henry's. In this we are heartily in accord with Fricke. Incidentally, Robert Elmer has the largest collection of choice and assorted pipes at present in captivity, and a taste in cats surpassing that of certain justly celebrated members of the Class. His huge angora, "Eveleth," named after a favorite professor, is a familiar sight in the Y. M. C. A. lobby, where circumambient purists often stop to stroke its beard. Another little maltese, "Vincent," has also won several prizes in local contests. It is, however, difficult to keep the striped rascal awake during the inspection by the judges. We shall refrain from all reference to Park Heights Avenue.

name: Friedenwald, Jonas Stein
motto: "You know physics, something of geology, Mathematics are your pastime."---*Browning*. "Half-grown as yet---a child, and vain."---*Tennyson*.
description: Cold and heartless, deliberately unfriendly, as it may seem, we beg leave to describe Friedenwald through the medium of a mathematical equation. Excellent student minus overwhelming realization of his own abilities plus the instincts of a true gentleman plus the spirit of good fellowship equals Jonas. In the great shift of unrealities he is a solid, confidence-inspiring figure. One has always the feeling that he is distinctly alive and human---which is no mean distinction in these decadent and parlous days. Jonas frankly admits his talents, but for this we cannot conscientiously censure him, as we are rather fond of ourselves. His belief in his essential infallibility cannot help seeming a bit strange to us, however, for he has been guilty of the greatest and most damning of absurdities. He is firmly convinced of the utter seriousness of Logic, and spends a good deal of valuable time in working out unbeatable combinations to spring on the emotional lecturer on the subject. Jonas shines in Philosophy, and may be said in some respects to have a long lead on his distinguished former classmate E. F. G. H. I. J. K. L. M. N. O. P. Q. R. Smith. Smith, however, makes up in green velour hats what he lacks in speed. There is a tradition in the Friedenwald family that every first son of a first son must of necessity become a physician. Jonas is following this unwritten law with the greatest exactness, and will furl his shingle to the breeze precisely four years from now. We trust and believe that he will make a most admirable wielder of stethoscope and scalpel---which may mean any one of a great number of things, but probably doesn't.

name: Manuel G. Gichner
motto: "In his brain Which is as dry as the remainder biscuit After a voyage—he hath strange places crammed With observations, the which he vents In mangled forms."—Shakespeare.
description: Manuel's unquestioned rank as an Evil Influence came soon to be whispered about in Chapel and barber shop. Freshmen regarded him reverently as a wholesale consumer of caramels and tea. Upperclassmen respected him for the following dictum, sprung on an unprepared and newly-landed Slonimsky, "Socrates was a squat, bow-legged Greek with a taste for hemlock—there ain't no answer." While this reasoning was unquestionably suffering from an undistributed middle, it was hailed by the student body with the greatest acclaim, and will doubtless file down in Hopkins tradition by the side of the "Ma" Greene whistle. Gichner's best friend is a pet toad-fish which splashes about in the Biology Lab. in the rare intervals when Manuel is attending a class, and accompanies him everywhere during the remainder of the day. The two are a familiar sight on Lexington Street—the dancer striding along at a swaying amble, the fish hopping in Frickeish glee behind. The chance remark that Havelok's pet resembled Coffin led to a division and vote in the Class. The results follow: Bryan, 18; Tingley, 17; Vickers, 5; Coffin, 3; Gichner (Vote of Fish.), 1; Leithiser, 1.

name: Hale, Robert W.
motto: "Want you a man Experienced in the world and its affairs? Here he is for your purpose."—Scott.
description: Hale and young Lochinvar both came out of the West, but from that point on Robert William has it all over his illustrious prototype. Vague recollections of Eng. Lit. III lead us to believe that the Scotchman was satisfied with taking a brief bath in the Eske. The rivers in Hale's native Ohio, however, have the habit of becoming dissatisfied with their surroundings, and seeking regions new on alternate week-ends, for which reason the sturdy yeomanry of thereabouts are compelled to lead a more or less amphibious existence. An unconfirmed rumor even has it that Hale is provided with webbed feet, and that this fact alone saved his life on one occasion when he was very young. The Editors are inclined to discredit this charming story, on the ground that, in all probability, Hale was never very young. For frequenters of the Maryland, Farson's, and the Jardin de Danse any account of Hale and his activities will, of course, prove superfluous. The present write-up is included in the book only that 1916 habituesses of Levering Hall, (for, we blush to admit, there are such), may learn a few useful facts about their classmate, and be provided, at the same time, with a "horrible example" of the results of leading the life unregenerate. For Hale, it must be confessed, lives with Fricke, Billings and Williamson, at the Y. M. C. A. He furthermore smokes Makaroffs with an abandoned air when fair damozels are in the immediate vicinity and carries, among other things, chorus girls' pictures and twenty-dollar gold-pieces. We can understand their giving him photographs. But girls, O girls, when you know the life he leads, why do you insist on giving him the twenty-dollar gold-pieces? Hale is a "man-about-town" by courtesy of Williamson's Overland Roadster.

name: Arthur A. Hardy
motto: "Let me play the fool, With mirth and laughter let old wrinkles come." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Member Class Baseball, Lacrosse and Track Teams, F., So.
description: Who can say that the original American humor is gone, when Hardy is an existing proof to the contrary? Combining Attic salt with Taurus Mexicanus, he sums up in his little personality the qualities of Mark Twain, Touchstone and George Fitch. He is funnier than Bert Williams, more laughable than Chaplin, Charles, and wittier than Nat Wills at his wildest. To hear him talk is a privilege and favor accorded to but few. To be admitted to his inmost confidences is a liberal education. For three years, we regret to say, Hardy confined his magnetic attentions to Shaw, Hale and Stargardter. We are glad to report now, however, that a little group of truth-seekers of both sexes and many colors comes together three nights a week at the Baltimore City College and listens with rapt interest to Hardy's lectures on English. His choice of subject caused some small surprise even to his closest intimates, for it had never been commonly understood that Hardy possessed a speaking knowledge of the mother tongue. His example has, however, proved beneficial in leading Knipp and Hilgartner to consider becoming instructors in Physics at the Mt. Vernon Collegiate Institute, while Penniman has also spoken of establishing a select class in French conversation. Hardy has won some degree of distinction in Economics, his Socio-Political definition of the French maximum-minimum tariff being worthy of special mention.

name: Hayes, E. Pearce
motto:
"Chaste as the icicle
That's curdled by the frost of purest snow
And hangs on Dian's temple."—Shakespeare.
"His virtue awful and prodigious."—Shelley.
clubs: Member Varsity Track Squad, F., So., J.
Member Glee Club, So., J., S.
President of Y. M. C. A., S.
description: People who don't know Pearce travel around under the false impression that his chief aim in life is to enter the ministry. Those a little further toward the inside of the track discover that he has also a deep abiding interest in politics, and even affects a careless Jeffersonian Democracy. Finally there are the few and fortunate who really know Hayes from long acquaintance and diligent watching. They will tell you that his two great aims in life are to strengthen an abnormally black and heavy beard, and to acquire more and yet more of a certain sugar-coated and innocuous bun put upon the market by the MacKenzie Bakery. We have so far failed to remark the fact that Hayes is very fond indeed of the female of the species, and is quite willing to take a chance concerning that virile Kiplingian “deadlier than the male” stuff. We shall never forget an afternoon at Homewood when, in his Sophomore year, Pearce took at least thirty-four capable Goucherettes to one of the football games. Hale and Rutledge filed up and down before the stand for two hours, bowing assiduously on every passage, and praying inwardly for a general introduction. Hayes was obviously impressed by the friendship displayed in their actions, but he limited his response to cordial nods. Later he apologized, assuring them that only the necessity of escorting the young ladies had prevented him from recognizing their salutes in a more friendly manner. The voice that Hayes airs in the Glee Club is a dreary and monotonous bass.

name: Helfrich, Oregon Benson
motto:
"His brow with thought was 'sicklied o'er,' "
We rarely saw him smile
And, e'en when none was looking on
His air was always woe-begone." --- Dobson.
"Ye air sae grave, nae doubt ye're wise." --- Burns.
clubs: Secretary and Treasurer of Chess Club, J.
description: HELFRICH does not yet wear his long trousers with abandon and an easy familiarity; the razor is to him an object dreaded but unknown. Yet as a prodigy of youthful learning he is unrivaled. Not even Dryden has the bug on him. He is the Boy Wonder of the Class. Don't look at him. We mean it. Morally, as intellectually, he is the superior of Hugh Latimer. Dryden is care-free as a potato-bug or seven-year cicada—Helfrich maintains through the most riotous of parties an air of funeral gloom. It is not a violent, iconoclastic cynicism, sprung from too much tobacco and Swinburne, such as affects certain of our number, nor yet a hopeless pessimism, as is that of janitorial George. Rather shall we call it a profound scholastic melancholy, a patient sadness born of over-much reflection on the flighty follies of a pleasure-driven world. Helfrich is a potential Luther. Woe betide the iniquitous if ever the vials of his wrath be loosed, or spilled, or something. The Board itself is pure. We don't mind Oregon's wanting to save us from ourselves, but we do object to his bragging about it. A sort of influence for the more serious side of existence is positively exuded by this fair-haired youth. This isn't the effect of our avenging consciences, either. Even Doc Whitney is frequently forced to pause in his lectures, as if suspecting the presence in the room of a being more lofty, more ethereal. And who shall say that Ruggles has gone wrong, despite his well-known confession as to the conversation with the barkeeper? Helfrich is rather a capable young man. He graduated in three years.

name: Knipp, John Christian
motto:
"A little upright, pert, tart, tripping wight
And still his precious self his dear delight;
Who loves his own smart shadow in the streets
Better than e'er the fairest lad he meets."—Burns.
"A walking haberdashery."—Scott.
clubs: Varsity Lacrosse Team, F., So., S.
Delegate to Athletic Board, F., So., J.
Manager and Member Class Football Team, F., So.
description: The serious business of Knipp's life is trying his best to resemble the artistically arrayed young men featured in the House of Botchenheimer ads on the backs of the magazines. It may be said with only pardonable exaggeration that if John and Lionel Barrymore were leaning side by side against a bar, no one would notice Lionel. Can we go further? Tunis Nash and Beau Dean aren't in it. Coffin alone, and that only in his Senior year, is comparable, for sheer tweed and plaid effects, to this, the last of all the Christian Knipps. But John doesn't always appear as a spruce, well-barbered college-story "fusser." He cavorts about the lacrosse field, when conditions and black-lists are favorable, and exerts by no means silent influence upon the score. Finally, he is an advertiser. And, when John seeks ads, he is compelled to disguise himself in a pair of overalls, a passionate English cap, and a full set of Santa Claus whiskers borrowed from the Poli Players for the purpose. You see, the advertisers are also looking for John. They have observed, with praiseworthy acumen, that results are not following upon their little ventures as rapidly as was expected, and are around seeking vengeance, or an explanation, or at least for Knipp. Knipp being small, they very seldom find him. Some one once referred to Johnny as "Christian," but the experiment was unsuccessful. He smiled urbanely. His conscience assured him that he was not a Christian.

name: Ludington, G. Franklin
motto: "O why did God, Creator wise, that peopled highest heaven With spirit masculine, create at last This novelty on earth, this fair defect Of nature?"—Paradise Lost.
description: For two years G. Franklin was noted chiefly for his wasplike, La Spirite contour, his sylph-like gait, and his class-room habit of rolling out sonorous periods in a thunderous soprano. Then something happened. Maybe he fell in love. Or perhaps it was something important. Certain it is that Frank has changed to a distinctly prosperous-appearing individual, that he has cultivated the habit of occasionally knowing what he is talking about, and that he has rather pushed himself into the 1916 limelight. It all began when he got on the News-Letter Board. Frank began to report lectures with precision and something closely approximating punch. His zealous enthusiasm frequently led him to include in his account certain highly interesting conclusions of his own of which the lecturer had never thought, and the lecturer, hearing of this, would immediately subscribe to the publication, hoping thereby to ground himself more thoroughly in his subject. Next, Ludington began to appear on official occasions circumstantially attired as an usher—he cast aside certain companions of his highly orthodox youth and began to think for himself. He read several books—he became a bit liberal in his views. Only one vice was yet resident unto him. He professed an ardent admiration for one much-maligned Bill Kaiser. The Mooshish Roosevelt was also close to his soul. With regard to these little misfortunes, we predict a change of heart for Frank. We also presume to predict a change of voice and mustache.

name: Miles H. Mccormick
motto:
"Myself when young did eagerly frequent
Doctor and saint, and heard great argument
About it and about, but evermore
Came out by that same door wherein I went."
---Fitzgerald.
"Your language proves you still the child."---Tennyson.
description: This attenuated confrere of Brian Boru and Daniel O'Connell hails from the misty mid-region of Stroudsburg and the ghoul-haunted woodland of here. For Mac hasn't any home at all. He boards hither and yon now and then, and has been known to visit friends. But if one wanted to reach him in a hurry, the case would be a desperate one. Fortunately, Mac isn't the sort that is visited in a hurry. Mac has the most disarming smile that ever Kewpie wore or maiden wanted. It helped him get by with a wrist-watch for a year, and got him past Joe Ames with a flat 6. Another thing helped McCormick in Physics, though. Joe couldn't call his name, but only struck on all sides of him, while Mac sat with his fingers crossed and watched the hoodoo do its deadly work. On the one occasion when he actually recited, he explained the principle of the perfume atomizer with such precision and tact that Fergy and Granny Bliss were hurried in from the next room to do trench duty and reinforce the fast-retiring Ames. Mac is using his liberal and carefully cribbed education to the utmost in devising for himself and his numerous landladies various degrees of cruel and unusual punishments, ranging from the burning of Chinese incense in his room to the keeping of goldfish. The story of how Baz, one of the little fish, playfully swam down the water-pipe one day and had to be clipped out with a pair of pincers, much to the detriment of his tail and general contour, is too long to be properly recorded here.

name: C. Whitford McDowell
motto:
"Some servile Scot with murder in his eye." — Freneau.
"Ye gods, how he talked — what a torrent of sound
His hearers invaded, encompassed and drowned." — Dobson.
"That had a fyr-reed cherubinnes face." — Chaucer.
clubs: Member Banquet Committee, So., J., S.
Member Glee Club, So., J., S.
description: The present Senior Class is blessed with so many great men, and so few hard workers, that it is a true pleasure to be able to speak of Mac, who is both. Not only is he a reincarnation (excuse us for referring to carnations, Getz) of a not unknown Homeric hero, viz., namely, and to wit, Achilles, swift as to his pedal extremities, but he is also distinguished as a player at the ball, covering himself with week-end coats of dirt, if not with glory. Debate? You ought to hear Mac advise. He acted in this official suggestive capacity at our Junior debate, sitting on the platform clad in an impressive "soup and fish," and studiously looking up the British colonial situation in the index of Skeat's Chaucer. Mac really had the jump on Brinton, the Senior's adviser, for Jeff had only literature referring to the subject in question. You know what happened to the Seniors? Well, Mac helped us do it. He doesn't only advise, though — we wouldn't give you that impression. Mac has, on occasion, filled up his gills like a porpoise, become beetishly red about the face, tangoed from hoof to hoof invitingly in his passion, and let forth a tumultuous storm of Anglo-Saxon that led the timid Calvin to reconsider his dictum that the members of the Class could well afford to indulge in an inebrious "Coke" at the palace of the kittenish Doc Adams before presenting themselves for a session in Public Speaking II. Mac comes, you know, from the residential city where Josephus Daniels and other rough Democrats are now hanging out, making Huyler's a place to be avoided. This partially, we think, accounts for him. But what was said about his working holds.

name: Page Milburn, Jr.
motto: "He had as much imagination as a pint-pot." — Shelley.
description: On the evening of our Junior banquet, an evening marked by many prodigies, not the least of which was the springing by a godly member in high standing in the class of a decidedly risqué story, many of us saw for the first time the fat and elfin face that is smiling at you from the corner of this page. Milburn, who had just decided to join our number, proceeded with undue dispatch to procure a 1916 pin, and otherwise show he was a real live Junior, but only a few were convinced. The rest of us have persisted in regarding Page with a weird and sneaking suspicion that he is young—oh, very, very young. We don't believe he has as yet reached that exalted mental and moral state which one naturally associates with members of graduating classes. Physically, we know that he is immature. The pink, pink cheeks, the lips deprived of down. We wonder why Milburn always blushes guiltily immediately after singing: "We've got sand in our make-ups?" It was on diverse and unforgettable Geology trips that Page's star shone at its brightest. While he always strenuously insisted, of course, upon sleeping alone in a little room reserved for his private use, he was not so careful about his public appearances, and once, clad only in a wholly inadequate towel, haunted the hall in a Cumberland hotel for an hour looking for Ludington, whom he suspected of having snitched the royal B. V. D.'s. It later transpired that George Edwin Dorsey, Graduate Manager and several other things not so easy to print, had mistaken Milburn's garment for a shoe-cleaner, and the loss was immediately made good. It is reported that Page has since provided himself with an extra suit made of pink muslin, décolleté effect, and with Ferris waist attached. He is thus protected against the sport of chance and the thoughtless depredations of his neighbors.

name: Arthur C. Primrose
motto: "So let the dumbness of this image be My eloquence, and still interpret me." - Lanier.
clubs: Member Football Team, F., So., J., S.
description: Arthur Chipman attends to the strong-arm end of things for the Class, and is our official gridiron representative. As Field Marshal he directs unexpected spring drives, and can usually open an opposing line in much the same manner that a McCormick thresher lays low a formerly healthy crop of wheat. Unlike certain other great generals, he is keenly aware of the dramatic possibilities of any and all occasions, and can extemporize fluently when urged. His greatest triumph was perhaps an address delivered before the student body on the Wednesday preceding the St. John's game, when he remarked, with startling originality, and perfect Ralph Henry Barbour style, "Fellas—I just feel the old fighting blood surgin' in my veins." Several Freshmen looked inadvertently impressed, and Primrose continued his metaphorical flights with increasing dash and disregard of personal safety. His quickness of thought, too, is not confined to the chalk-marked green. As Freshman President he solved a difficulty that might well have baffled more renowned administrators. Class pins had to be selected at a certain meeting—the samples, when held on high by the Presidential arm, proved too small to be properly viewed. Was Primrose for a moment dismayed? Not at all—he described the pins to the Class, and voting was made simple enough for a child of seven. Otts possesses social gifts that must not pass unmentioned. As a dancer, as a raconteur, as a catch-as-catch-can wit, he is all but unsurpassed. A certain native capacity for attaching the affections of the fair sex does not make him a less dangerous and sinister individual. He should be handled with the greatest care.

name: Rosen, Raphael
motto:
"Then hail, late-comer to the festive board,
Pause, and make lightly merry."
description: No one ever suspected Rosen of intentions to graduate until a day or so before the HULLABALOO was supposed to go to press, but didn't, when he quietly submitted a biography and declared his desire to "stick around." We had seen and admired from a distance his magnificent imperviousness to Ethics, we had been a bit impressed by the fine lyric poet hirsutial equipment. But consider him one of us? Never in our wildest flights had such an idea presented itself. There are two things of especial note about Rosen. One is, the fine, emaciated, bust-of-Dante countenance which he affects, hinting at years of self-denial and patient endeavor, and touching the heart of the hardest of instructors. The other is his amazing gift for absorbing punishment in the way of work. The lad has a positive genius for tearing through tomes, for sitting in the shade of the professorial pulpit and taking reams of notes. All this he does with an unblinking gravity that cannot fail of its effect upon those about him. We remember that a brother of the youth in question once frequented these halls, and raised a most deceptive mustache on the very eve of graduation. "Eve" is of course used metaphorically, for it took him two months to raise the mustache. Whether the failure of the present hopeful to follow suit was due to lack of desire or to incapacity we are at a loss to determine. Certain it is that we appreciate his motives or the kindness of Nature. That bronzed medallion head graced by a Charlie Chaplin? Never, no, never.

name: Rosenthal, Emil
motto: "The over curious are not over wise."—Massinger.
description: Rosenthal may not be over-wise, but he is certainly over-educated. A week spent in his presence should qualify even Hardy for a Rhodes scholarship. Not that we dislike Hardy enough to condemn him to a straight week in Rosenthal's company. Alexander Rusk is a much better and more fit companion for him. Curiosity circles the youth's spine and is exuded from every pore of his being. Whenever a remark of any kind is made, sotto voce, in classroom, Emil turns to greet its author with a wide smile of sympathetic recognition and encouragement, even if the observation was only to the effect that the room was hot, cold or lukewarm, as it usually is. Since entering the Senior Class, he has been especially assiduous in attempting to convince the recognized Senior wits of his appreciation of their pithy sayings, and as a consequence they have come to regard him with an aversion bordering on dread. Like Dryden, Rosenthal has always the widest and most disagreeable of smiles, which gives his face a peculiarly fatuous expression. As it is unquestionably his face, however, such details should doubtless be left under his own control. Chemistry, Geology and Minerology are as nothing to him. For that matter, they are as nothing to most people. Paleontology he has not yet entered upon, but it is very possible that Fader may yet have a rival in this important field of Science. A horrible suspicion enters the Editorial mind at this juncture. Just Heaven, . . . . . . . . . . . . . .another doctor?

name: Shaw, Edwin Orville
motto: "He has never fed of the dainties that are bred in a book."—Shakespeare.
description: He is one of those large and expansive persons that Chen calls "demagogues," but reminds the Occidental observer more of the thick Teutonic gentleman who rolls it by the keg to Fritz's Kaif. When Shaw is dolled up in his blue and labish apron this effect is, of course, greatly increased. Shaw has been known to "spill one." We regret to remark that, like the dissolute Griz, he also takes tobacco both internally and externally—that is to say, by injection and ejection. His fair unblushing cheek is usually distended by a liberal section of Sailor's Delight, and the floor around his table in what Gichner calls the "Chem. Lab." has acquired a permanent brownish stain. Shaw is said, nevertheless, to be a great favorite with those of the predatory sex who "can't resist large men." As a dancer he surpasses Vermin Castle—as an all-night show-it-to-me beau he has the drop on Hale. As a student, however, Shaw is careful, rather than brilliant. The inability of Ed. the Griffin to understand his Western Maryland Latin on a given occasion did much to bring about this state of affairs, but a New England conscience and inborn ability to take punishment may be considered as major contributory causes. Since this disastrous experience, Shaw has reserved his bits of inflammatory knowledge for the little circle of savants, headed by Stargardter, which is known as the Hopkins Pleiad, and with which he is constantly associated. Shaw has made many friends in the course of his three years at Hopkins.

name: Spieker, Edmund Maute
motto: "Sang in tones of deep emotion Songs of love and songs of longing."—Longfellow. "Mine is the lay that lightly floats, And mine are the murmuring dying notes That fall as soft as snow on the sea."—Moore.
clubs: Member Varsity Glee Club, F., So., J., S.
Undergraduate Correspondent Alumni Magazine, So., J.
description: This is Edmund Maute. He wears his hair long and cultivates a dreamy expression, gazing out raptly into nothingness with those wonderful blue eyes of his, always imagining that some one will say in his hearing, "Ah me—his soul is music." Though there is no earthly reason why it shouldn't be concerned with editorial paste-tube, blue pencil and shears: Spieker's is the controlling voice in the direction of the News-Letter, as well as the star-searching soprano of the Glee Club. He is also a scholar of parts, though not over many, and was once reported to be able to do the hundred in ten flat. We are simply trying to convince you that Edmund Maute is rather a capable young man. Not that we owe him any money. You might not suspect it from merely casting a casual glance at his photo, but as an entertainer he is unsurpassed. He was a member of the Hopkins Committee that escorted certain California agricultural students hither and thither about the State of Maryland, and an interested listener at the ensuing supper when Tingley explained the meaning of the official seal, of whose existence both had hitherto been ignorant. Spieker is not only a great man, but a good fellow. He possesses many of the graces so strikingly absent from certain others in the Class who lead exemplary lives, but do little or nothing else. His favorite haunt is the New Howard, where he is usually to be found with Bell and a few friends of similar thirst and tendencies.

name: Stargardter, Albert R.
motto: "He was a mortal of the careless kind, With no great love of learning or the learned." —Byron.
description: He has put in more time assiduously cultivating the "evil eye" than Julian Eltynge devoted to the debutante slouch or Mary Pickford to her smile. He learned how to play the piano so that his fingers would look long and thin, like a gambler's. He sleeps all day so that he can stay out all night, doing nothing in particular, but bragging about it like the most depraved of sinners. He smokes thirty cigarettes half through every day to show that he is independent, and withal a rake. With women he is a demon. You know the type, girls. Long black hair, intense and passionate expression, languorous, flowing gestures with the thin, pale hands. He doesn't know the style he is affecting went out of fashion with East Lynne, that "new women" are falling for the carefully rehearsed springy stride of the athlete. Albert dances at the lowest of resorts, and has been known to drink a saucer of beer with the greatest gusto. This, too, is part of the stage business in the "going to hell like a skyrocket" game, and had, therefore, to be learned, even though it went against the Stargardterian nature to do so. We wonder if the person who took Albert to the movies for the first time doesn't regret it. It was there that he learned his "Perils of Pearline" walk and sigh, though the clever line of patter which he is ever ready to loose is doubtless picked up at the Garden, where all manner of things may be picked up, and a few caught for all time to come. Sox. is a bad, bad fellow. Frankly, we don't approve of him at all.

name: Tinges, Charles Howard
motto: "We grant, although he had much wit, He was very shy of using it, And therefore bore it not about, As being loath to wear it out."—Butler.
clubs: Assistant Manager Lacrosse Team, F., So.
Assistant Manager Football Team, So., J.
Member Class Baseball Team, F., So.
Member Class Track Team, F., So.
description: Many there be at Hopkins who are clever, but refuse to work; there is a yet larger number of strong men who deny their presence to track and gridiron, diamond and court, preferring the arm chair by the fire, or, at the most, the office of the manager. Tinges is one of the latter class. Occasionally, feeling restive after a particularly sleep-provoking session in History or Politics, he picks up a table or chair and tears it idly apart in his fingers. But this physical energy is short-lived. He is vastly fonder of arranging games for football teams or dances for cotillons. Charles is lazy. Like most fat and strong men, he is also essentially good-natured, and is possessed of a vein of humor that is far more spontaneous than are those of certain of our officially designated funny men. His powerful figure is always clad after the latest fashion, and his face is ever fresh from the barber's hands. Tinges has always been confused by Faculty and students alike with Tingley, greatly to the detriment of both. As a consequence, Charlie receives an occasional 10 for a recitation when the larger part of the mark is an unearned increment, and Tingley is frequently warned that he is skidding perilously close to the black-list. Fortunately, neither seems to greatly concern himself over the mistake, although it occasionally causes some confusion in the minds of those who are less directly interested. Tinges is a genial, compact and efficient man who is generally liked, under one name or the other. Wherever he goes, he will be good company, and make the best of friends.

name: Thomas Jay Tingley
motto:
"He knows the policies of foreign lands,
Can string you names of districts, cities, towns
The whole world over."—Wordsworth.
"His talk is like a stream which runs—
He slips from politics to puns."—Praed.
clubs: Member Varsity Debating Team, J., S.
Member Debating Council, So., J., S.
description: There are two sorts of mysteries. One is the common or garden variety which one looks upon, recognizes for what it is, and solves or leaves unsolved, as the case may be. The other is a genuine problem. From the first, one cannot determine whether or not it is mysterious. Thomas Jay Tingley is a fair example of this latter class. Only to the cultured few is it given to see the broad and scrupulously shaven countenance of our President as seven obscuring veils, to recognize his open and ingenuous manner as a dark disguise. Lest this assertion be doubted, let us call attention to the man's conversation. Do the strange animal noises that punctuate his discourse show only a youthful exuberance of spirits, a vernal fullness of soul, or are they the ritual of a bestial egromancy, the aberrations of a genius amounting almost to madness? For such lovers of the esoteric and undivulged as this sketch may chance to reach, let me propound the following queries, tending to establish the artless candor or the insidious concealment of our subject: 1. Why does Tingley always get what he goes after? 2. Is there a causal nexus between whiskers and achievement? 3. Does he get away with it? If so, how? 4. Does Tingley really know anything about cats? 5. What does Tingley think of his classmates, the Faculty, et al.? 6. Is the Evil Influence an eleemosynary institution? 7. What does he want? When he gets it, will he have won or lost?

name: Barron, David Howard
motto: "In mathematics he was greater Than Tycho Brahe or Erra Pater."
description: SELDOM indeed does this article of furniture favor us with the light of what he doubtless calls a countenance, but we can remember better days, when things were different. Barron wasn't always an unconsulted engineer, a base mechanic. Well do we recall him as the ornament of the Modern Language Department, swimming to the ears in so-called "cultural courses," the favorite of Blossom, the deserved of all deservers. Barron had a reputation, at that time, of being able to cripple the King's English more per square inch than could anyone else per yard or Hopkins campus. "Horse—one of them things with legs what runs." Could anything be clearer? Notice also that Barron cleverly differentiated here between the common or garden variety horse, which can, and ought to, run, and the gymnasium "horse," which can't, and had better not try. Barron doesn't only kill more English than Von Hindenburg did at his high—Pilsner mark—he also grows. We don't at all mean to infer that he takes on a gentlemanly inch a year. He increases by heaps and pounds; whether by taking thought as to his stature (this will be over Fricke's head), or "just growin'," we are at a loss to determine. Suffice it to say that in a few years he should prove quite a capable helper around the store. We regret that Barron took Logic out at Homewood, as we should have liked to hear him prove that All S is P or otherwise. Here's hoping that the Associated Order of Pipe-Fitters never ask him to address them at their annual banquet.

name: Cockey, James P.
motto: "Grave as a shaven clerk, By this sign shall ye know them— That they ruin and make dark."—Chesterton.
description: Cockey is the official bean-spiller of the University. His bland, inconsequential, ceaseless line of select conversation could be relied upon to embarrass three administrations, precipitate a war or so, and lose at least eight friends in the course of any given twenty-four hours. The beauty of it all is that Cockey never realizes the horrible effect of his faux mot, to use an Engineering phrase, even when the building is blown up and the cat is dead. He looks about him in a pleased, dazed way, and finds some one else to ruin. It is generally understood that one of the governments now engaged in war is anxious to annex the services of promising young men who combine diplomatic graces with a fiendish knowledge of things technical and mechanical. No Hopkins Employment Bureau would ever hesitate a moment to recommend this young man for such a situation. He has the finesse of a Bernhardi, the engineering skill of a Rupert Brooke, the devastating force of a tornado. Perhaps it is fortunate that James' smile is bland and his manner disarming; it may be well that his holocaustic breath is directed toward the destruction of the aesthetic, rather than the physical. The neck obtruding above the painfully clean collar in the cut above is alarmingly large and rough. What could Cockey accomplish if he were once really launched upon a career of crime? This question we must leave unanswered. He must remain as he is—no more, no less. We invite your most attentive consideration of his talents and peculiarities.

name: Raymond Wilson Evitt
motto: "He was a lovely youth. I guess The panther in his wilderness Was not so fair as he."--Wordsworth.
description: Score up another embryo Goethals, landlord. For Evitt, assuredly a man of parts and potentialities, has secreted himself for two years in the bosky dells of Homewood, chatting familiarly with his boon companion, Woodward, of dynes and foot-pound pressures, and speaking of bridges with familiarity and abandon. Eagerly he pores over, in the dim watches of the fleeting night, the "fairy tales of science." We occasionally suspect Evitt of having a highly technical "mission"--of being, in short, an Aler to the mechanical world--but this has never been proved against him. Certainly he would make a charming prophet of the H. G. Wells type, inventing highly supposititious woozlebusses and amblesquids on every other fortnight. You see there is a future for Evitt. Once in a long time the wholly efficient dreamer emerges from his cavern and wanders forth to see how the other small fraction of the world is conducting itself. On such occasions he takes his way into McCoy Hall, finds someone to engage in conversation, usually selecting Louie Naylor because the latter never dares to contradict or talk back, and is soon caught in the act of retreating to his lair again for a long period of hibernization. We are led to believe that this is a common practice among engineers. Evitt has classical, as well as technical, attainments. His translation of "Frau Sorge" is still the standard edition, as well as the only undeleted one. His French is that of Provence—his Latin is hard to nail down to any nation, school, or influence. In all our Class photos that have been taken up to date he can be seen in the front row, filling a by no means insignificant portion of the perpetuation of our collective likeness.

name: Ulric O. Hutton
motto: "He rejoiceth as a strong man to run a race." —Psalm 19.
clubs: Member Track Team, So., J., S.
description: After the toil and stress of our Freshman year, we entered the Sophomore season riotously, and counted over our numbers with confidence. We were surprised to find among those numbers one morning the figure of Ulric Orlando Hutton, who had appeared suddenly as from nowhere, and whom we trust never to lose in the same fashion. Hutton was a member of the Class from the very start. He was one of our hazing party, and suffered the fate of 1916 in general. Before we were reinstated, his genial smile and ready good-fellowship had become familiar, and there were few indeed among us who did not know and like him. Hutton is a trotter of no mean ability, and can circle a half mile in a manner that recalls the good old days when Alfred Salem Niles was doing distance work. But it is as a philosopher and formal logician that he really shines. Not satisfied with classroom opportunities to hunt out and expose insidious illicit majors and negative premises, Hutton frequently drops around to call on Slony in his Howard Street lair, and the two riddle petitio principiis to their hearts' content, and until morning gilds the skies. The thoughtful observer cannot see them thus engaged without thinking of the famous historical parallel of their strange association—that between Socrates and Plato. Who knows that the Slonimsky Inverse may not be followed by the Hutton Contrapostive? The discovery of the Firor Dilemma is of course familiar to our readers. Strange, strange that this new Force, this Flash of Light from Odessa, should so soon have made its power manifest among us, molding the lives of Stargardter, Hutton and the rest so readily. We trust only that its influence will in the long run prove to have been beneficial.

name: Fred. T. Iddings
motto:
"He was short-shouldered, broad, a thicke knarre,
Ther nas no dore that he nolde heve of harre."
—Chaucer.
description: If bulls will insist on straying into china shops, and musculous, athletic individuals into Engineering Schools, they must not object to being reverenced, and photographed, and displayed to an admiring multitude of readers of the 1916 Hullabaloo. The rough, uneven country about Homewood seems to have a by no means negligible effect on the physique and temper of the low mechanics who there squint through surveying instruments, and make marks with blue chalk with the utmost abandon. Several perfectly nice chaps who changed their habitat after the Sophomore year have developed, under this sinister influence, into quite terrifying individuals. Not so with Iddings, however. He was a power from the first, and subsequent events have only accentuated and increased his capacities. Iddings graciously refrained from tearing down the new Tech. Buildings and changing the appearance of the adjacent landscape, preferring, instead to vault, graceful and fawn-like, over the thin willow pole, taking chances at breaking records and necks and things. No one present to witness him in the act of indulging in this favorite amusement is ever unimpressed, with the single exception of Iddings himself. He takes the entire affair in a tired, bored manner that is reminiscent of Aler's attitude toward soul-snatching. It is business—nothing more or less. Iddings is a quiet young man, and all information regarding him is based on observation or surmise. We feel justified in making the assumption that he is good-natured and kind to those about him, like all other strong men, and probably possessed of all manner of subsidiary virtues of which no one has ever even suspected him.

name: David Johnston
description: JOHNSTON is a cool, clean-cut, aggressive young man of the type that one would like to encourage, but cannot afford to, because of the keen strenuousness of the economic and biological struggle for existence. He is the combination of result-getter and likable fellow that usually comes back in the third act and marries the girl. His success as a student is only surpassed by his amazing authority in the role of party "whip." No more of this, however. It is unfortunate that Johnston was called away to Homewood immediately after his Sophomore year, for he gave every promise of meriting intensive cultivation at close range. He was a quiet but active participant in all the wild adventures attendant upon the Hazing Bee, and an honorable unit of the group that was officially dished for some weeks thereafter. The removal to join the tech. squad was also unfortunate in that it involved the severance of a Platonic friendship, conceived in German I (where many strange things are born, and infinitely more come to a grievous end), with John Christian Knipp. Knipp sought for a time to follow his intellectual confrere, but unfortunately confused the term "foot-pound" with a well-known corncure on the Christmas exam., and was compelled to return to Group III, where anything can get by. Johnston is a strong man out of the West by potentiality, an engineering student of no mean degree of ability by choice, and a gentleman by instinct. It is because of these facts that we regard him with the most respectful veneration.

name: Leithiser, Samuel L.
motto: "Ah me, in sooth he was a shameless wight Sore given to revels and ungodly glee."—Byron.
description: Strange, paradoxical, utterly unbelievable as it may seem, Leity actually lives, as far as this is physically possible, in the flourishing manufacturing center of Havre de Grace. As often, however, as the desire for a dish of beer and a Menkenized "Sun paper" become too strong for him, he commandeers a Pennsy Wrecker and invades these parts in all the glory of his rustic make-up. It might be said in justice to the subject under discussion that he has a pass on the road, conferred in compensation for feudal services rendered by some forgotten ancestor, so that the trip is expensive only in time, which is nothing in Leity's language. Of course, S. L. couldn't be called a Vernon Chesterfield, or a Beau Castle, or anything of that sort, but the electorate in the little borough just beyond the Susquehanna Bridge are both impressed with his daring English plaids and defiant glasses a la Wilfred Pirt, and a Parliamentary career may await Leithiser after his graduation. The natural question immediately arising is: When will Leithiser graduate? For although he cultivates a studious expression and a cloistered mustache, although he takes exams and re-exams as if he thought nothing of them, and even though he makes some claims at being, with Tingley and Gichner, a connoisseur in cats, Leithiser has troubles intellectual and aesthetic that are all his own. Through them all, however, it must be said that he preserves the sunniest and most even of tempers, the calmest and most unruffled of dispositions. The rush and worry incidental upon traveling often render him a bit peevish and irritable in the extremely early morning, it is true, but it is seldom indeed that he does not get a little more cordial during the course of the day. In other words, he is usually "up to the pinch."

name: Henry Rowland
motto:
"How many a time have I
Cloven with arm still lustier, breast more daring
The wave all roughened; with a swimmer's stroke
Flung the billows back from my drenched hair!"
—Byron.
description: Tall and gaunt is he, and in his eyes is an inexplicable shadow which suggests something eerie, something ghost-like. Every movement is a model of deliberate dignity. Only when Henry swims does the great frigid mask wear through a bit; he becomes unmistakably red in the face, and breathes rapidly through open mouth in pollywoggian haste and greediness. This, you will doubtless admit, is no behavior for a gloomy and dignified man. Rarely is Rowland seen in the company or in the haunts of his fellow-students. Rarely has he been known to voice an opinion—or much of anything else. So far removed does he seem from human tendencies and human weaknesses that he might readily be thought an inhabitant of another sphere. Aesthetic, is far too weak a word to describe him. He is, indeed, a figure shrouded in mystery. So, as might be expected, little is known of the character, romantically and intangibly, yet none the less effectively, veiled. Can it be that his natural, friendly instincts are overwhelmed by the weight of an awful secret in a tragic past? Or has the Class of 1916 unconsciously been harboring and misunderstanding a chrysalidal genius? Be these things as they may, at present, "Henry" is synonymous with "enigma."

name: Abraham Tobias
motto: "Remote, unfriendly, melancholy, slow."—Goldsmith. "Grave, thoughtful and reserved among his mates."—Scott.
description: There are an infinite number of reasons for any man's coming to college, and an infinite number of things that college can give him after he gets there. Tobias chose from the first to devote himself to studying, and studying hard—this he has continued in throughout unwaveringly, following a steady policy and getting equally stable results. His marks are always of the highest, but he has never been known to "boot-lick," or "whine for a raise," as have some of his more ostentatious classmates. "Toby" cannot be laughed down or overlooked. In his own way, he demands recognition. Tobias has no intimates at the University, and has never been observed to seek any. He seems to take the greatest of interest in his own affairs, and none at all in those of others. He is not, however, a preoccupied grouch, and never fails to speak to a classmate in passing. Finally, he has no enemies, while there are many who sincerely respect him. Tobias never failed to attend a class meeting when he was studying in McCoy Hall, and has always been interested in the welfare of 1916. We wish him the greatest success as he graduates from Hopkins.

name: Vickers, Luke R.
motto:
"Sharp-kneed, sharp-elbowed, and lean-ankled, too,
With long and ghostly shanks."—Wordsworth.
description: FIGURATIVELY speaking, Rube is in a class by himself. He is a long, thin racing model of the U-29 type, combining the dexterity and cunning of the shark with a certain lissomeness and grace that are commonly associated with what Barron might call (but had better not), a female goddess. It is this latter quality that has won for him the pseudonym of "Venus." Rube rather likes the name himself, and has frequently been known, tear in eye and with a measurable degree of asperity, indignantly to admit its applicability. There are people in this world—residing on the Eastern Shore, to be exact—who think Rube is an engineer. Fortunately, the Board of Editors and the Hopkins Faculty labor under no such delusion. When it comes to photography, however, the case is altered a bit. The work of Vickers at the Sophomore banquet, when the hostile and indignant Gichner was performing in his cabaret feature de luxe, and on the field of Homewood, that unforgettable and chilly evening of November 7, will always be appreciated by the members of the 1916 Class. Later on, when he is snapping war stuff for the "London Times" to illustrate Scarborough's features, we shall look back with pride, remarking, "Uh-huh—we raised him from a pup. He used to smoke our cigarettes reg'lar." You saw a mouse-skin sombrero running the side-lines at the games this season? That was Rube, handling the photographic work for the HULLABALOO. Not up to Coffin on dress or Leity on cordiality, perhaps, but rather a business-like young man, and in every sense of the word a sure result-getter.

name: Webster, James A.
motto: "Strange is thy pallor, strange thy dress, Strange above all thy length of tress."—Poe.
description: The careless curly hair, caught and tossed and constantly caressed by the breezes of summer, winter, and sundry intermediate seasons, is suggestive of Byron; right there the similarity ends. Webster's only poetical venture was the publication of a few sonnets to Johnston during the first stages of their romantic infatuation. Johnston says he intends to become married immediately after graduation. Can it be that there is a heart-break—excuse us, Gich—a heart-break—in store for James? We don't know that he could stand seeing the bottom of his passion-plunge. Webster, say what you will, is at least possessed of the courage of his convictions. Considering that an allusion cast upon his friendship with Johnston was slighting, he astonished the alluder and everyone else at the Sophomore banquet by remarking in a low, tense voice, "Yes, I like him. What are you going to do about it?" No one did anything. We haven't seen much of Webster for the last two years, as he was "among those missing" after the Homewood Engineering contingent had been formed. We are quite ready to admit that he was rather an excellent young man when last viewed about these precincts, but that Engineering School has had a distinctly unfortunate effect upon several of our number who had made very acceptable Freshmen and Sophomores, and as a result we advise all who meet him to form their own conclusions. One thing is certain—his name is never among those of the many who regularly and monotonously flunk out on Christmas, Easter and Final Exams. And this, of course, is more or less of a distinction in itself.

name: Wood, Walter A.
motto: "And could I love another? No, no, no!"—Old Song
description: This man Paul Poiret might as well hurry the thing along and be killed by the Germans, instead of getting sick-leave every week and having his handsome likeness in the Sunday Supplement of the New York Times. Fritz has him on style from the word "go." His effects are always striking and occasionally correct; we have yet to see him when his appearance will be uninteresting to the morbidly inquisitive. He knows precisely what type of smoking-jacket to wear to an evening wedding, and would never consider going to an early morning class in anything short of a frock-coat, white flannels and pearl-colored spats. His neckwear is ever of the wildest. Wood's work as Art Manager of the Hullabaloo must be allowed to speak for itself, and we trust it will prove sufficient to divert attention from certain other features of the book, such as Gichner's picture. He is an athlete of some distinction, and, in his Freshman year, used, if we remember correctly, to soar over a cross-bar in persistent flights toward the ceiling, while every one else watched the gymnasium clock, and prayed for the finishing bell. We suspect him of being one of those social lions who goes to dances every other night, and comes home the next morning via the early Lutherville car. Certain it is that he wouldn't be an inch above it. Look out for Wood in your various prowls about the city streets, and when you see the afore-time-hinted-at scenic effects coupled with an alarming shock of taffy-colored hair and a winning geniality of approach, you will know that you have discovered a man distinctly worth the knowing.

name: Woodward, Hiram W.
motto: "I am not in the roll of common men."—*Shakespeare*. "And by proof of balanced answer I decide that I am best."—*Swinburne*.
clubs: Associate Editor News-Letter, F., So., J., S.
Treasurer Y. M. C. A., J.
Manager Varsity Lacrosse Team, J., S.
description: WOODWARD is as steady and efficient a worker as the Class of 1916 can boast. His triumphs are not all of a spectacular variety, but he has done as much as any other man to make the Graduating Class an active force in Hopkins undergraduate affairs. His work on the Student Council did much to bring that body to the position of dignity and influence which it now occupies; as manager of the lacrosse team, too, he performed yeoman’s service, the value of which cannot well be overestimated. As a member of committees and of athletic teams, he has been an active participant in 1916 affairs from the time of the organization of the Class. The part he played in the flurried inter-class revolutions of Freshman and Sophomore years was both brilliant and daring, and will not soon be forgotten. It is not to be inferred that Hiram’s triumphs are confined to executive and administrative lines. Certain of his German translations, notably that of “Aus dem Leben eines Taugenichts,” have become classics through their pearly perfection of phrase and incisively appreciative criticism. As a silver-tongued Leader of the Opposition, too, he is valuable in holding up class meetings to interpose pithy jests and apropos objections. He can furthermore be relied upon to inject an element of uncertainty into any vote that may be taken by producing from his pocket scores of neatly typed proxies from the Engineering School, which always seems, when absent, to be unanimous in his support. Aside from these things, Hiram is, in some respects, a thoroughly normal individual. We wish him godspeed on his highly interesting and individualistic career.
